insight: Buying two Arms Race units back-to-back is generally poor strategy; the first Arms Race is solid but sequential purchases quickly lose value. The efficiency of Arms Race depends heavily on whether your opponent's Engineers are forced to sit idle, generating virtual gold advantage for each useless Engineer turn.
author: consensus
context: Discussion about Arms Race usage and how many to buy in a given game.

insight: Arms Race is efficient when your opponent cannot immediately use their Engineers next turn (i.e., they are over-defended). Once the opponent is no longer forced to waste Engineers, additional Arms Races lose their discount advantage and become merely an alternate cost rather than a true discount.
author: supergreyman
context: Thread analyzing Arms Race value and when it stops being efficient.

insight: If you buy one Arms Race you should usually buy at least two, because the second Arms Race compounds the over-defense on your opponent, forcing their Engineers to sit idle even longer and spending your tech efficiently.
author: lyra1712
context: Discussion about optimal number of Arms Race purchases and their cumulative effect.

insight: Once Arms Race is generating full absorb value against the opponent, Colossus becomes the preferred follow-up attacker. Typically only 2 Arms Races are bought in Colossus-Arms Race strategies because damage ramps so fast that all 4 Colossi may not even be reachable (2 Arms Race + 3 Colossi already equals 15 damage).
author: consensus
context: Discussion about Arms Race into Colossus transition strategies.

insight: Centurion is considered extremely efficient as an absorber, effectively functioning as though it has 8 health because its 2 absorb negates 2 incoming attack per turn — a heuristic that treating 1 absorb as equivalent to 1 attack for comparison purposes, though with caveats about timing and tempo.
author: f300xen
context: Debate over whether Centurion is a must-buy and how to evaluate absorb vs. health.

insight: A useful heuristic is that 1 damage is worth approximately 2 gold, and 1 absorb is approximately equal to 1 attack in value — making absorbers with both health and absorb (like Centurion) significantly more efficient than raw health units. However, this approximation degrades for units with low attack-to-health ratios.
author: totally_not_fbi
context: Discussion of unit valuation heuristics in the context of comparing absorbers.

insight: Rhino absorbing for only 1 when you are already over-defended is very bad — even worse than buying a Perforator that doesn't absorb at all — because the absorb provides no real value when excess defense already covers the incoming attack.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion of responses to Arms Race, specifically whether absorbing on Rhino is worth it when already over-defended.

insight: Odin with Energy Matrix creates a gambit situation: holding Odin forces the opponent to defend for 4 extra threat, and if they click their Odin to force you to soak yours, you can still absorb on Energy Matrix instead. The two correct options with Odin are: attack with it every turn, or hold it and sacrifice it to absorb on Matrix. Holding and over-defending with Odin is the worst option.
author: consensus
context: Extended discussion about optimal Odin (Steelsplitter clicking) play when paired with Energy Matrix.

insight: Odin (Steelsplitter) is considered potentially broken because it is heavily P1-favored: whoever gets Odin first typically gets it multiple turns before the opponent can respond, since the opponent must immediately defend 7 threat. This snowballs into a significant tempo advantage for the first buyer.
author: namington
context: Discussion prompted by Msven streaming and calling Odin broken; community analyzes whether this is accurate.

insight: Cauterizer is not a good way to initiate permanent attack because at 11BRR it is expensive for only 2 attack; its value comes from doubling as a defender. The correct time to buy Cauterizer is when transitioning from building attack to building defense, not as an early attack unit. Tarsiers and Iceblades are better early attackers in most cases.
author: consensus
context: Discussion analyzing a game where Cauterizer was bought as an early attacker.

insight: Savior is viable in many sets beyond just the early rush timing — a later Savior can be very powerful. The dominant opinion that Savior was unviable has been revised; it may be a top-tier unit in a wide range of sets.
author: consensus
context: Discussion about Savior's viability after community opinion shifted from considering it unviable.

insight: Zemora Voidbringer's DC/DC/D1 P2 rush opening is only good if the opponent does not go Zemora in response; it should be used in non-obvious Zemora sets or against weaker players. If Zemora is generally strong in a set and the opponent doesn't respond, a slower Zemora line (DD DDC) is preferable to the rushed P2 DC opening.
author: crash_overlord
context: Discussion about optimal P2 Zemora opening lines and when the fast DC rush is correct.

insight: Tia Threnody is a strong strategy when the opponent lacks good soak, and it is very hard to defend without good defensive options. Mirroring the Tia buy (getting Tia first) can beat a counter-Tia response. Natural Animus into Tarsiers is risky when the opponent has Tia available.
author: willflame
context: Post-game analysis of a Tia Threnody win, evaluating what the losing player could have done differently.

insight: Centurion makes Endotherm Kit significantly stronger because it enables a Gambit Centurion strategy that acts as a breachproof line against freeze. Without Centurion (or another gambit unit like D-Grid), beating Endotherm Kit typically requires going all-red and not defending rather than trying to out-wall all the Flakes.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion about how to beat Endotherm Kit and what makes it stronger or weaker.

insight: Amporilla (Corpus/Animus combo) timing is critical — going Ampo too early or too late loses, but the window to Ampo exists in most sets that include it. One player going Ampo while the other does not tends to result in the non-Ampo player winning approximately 80% of the time at high levels, making mutual Ampo commitment the dominant line.
author: kristian1103
context: Discussion about Amporilla strategy and game-warping impact in sets where it appears.

insight: In base+Tia sets, P2's DDC DA opening beats all non-third-Engineer lines. Base+Tia is effectively a third-Engineer set, and the standard third-Engineer counter line (DD DDE DDD DDDC with Engineer as the last buy) beats P2's DDC DA.
author: f300xen
context: Discussion about Zemora and Tia openings, where fox reveals base+Tia counts as a third-Engineer set.

insight: Valk (Valkyrie) is a strong first attacker because it gets over the absorb barrier, denies absorb while giving good tempo early, and transitions into a regular attacker later. The sequence Valk > hold Valk while making other purchases > second Valk is a strong pattern, and if there is no better absorber than Wall in the set, holding Valk is essentially like having an I-Grid that threatens 4.
author: consensus
context: Extended discussion about Valk (Valkyrie) usage, timing, and optimal buying patterns.

insight: In Cynestra sets, Cynestra is almost always correct to purchase (except against specific aggressive units). Cynestra is a very efficient attacker that tends to compress game timelines, making Arms Race strategies less viable because the game ends too fast for them to pay off.
author: shadourow
context: Set analysis discussion involving Cynestra, Arms Race, and Mahar, evaluating which strategies are viable.

insight: Doomed Mech is especially strong later in the game because it provides both defensive value on the final turn and absorb, making it better than Tarsiers as a late-game attacker when you need to transition to building defense.
author: 1m2o
context: Discussion about which attackers are best in the late game when holding Valk no longer denies absorb.
